message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  We apply our mechanistic, within-host,
                  <jats:italic>pre-immunity</jats:italic>
                  , respiratory tract infection model for unvaccinated, previously uninfected, and immune-compromised individuals. Starting from published cell infection and viral replication data for the SARS-CoV-2 alpha variant, we explore variability in outcomes of viral load and cell infection due to three plausible mechanisms altered by SARS-CoV-2 mutations of delta and omicron. We seek a mechanistic explanation of clinical test results: delta nasal infections express ∼3 orders-of-magnitude higher viral load than alpha, while omicron infections express an additional 1 to 2 orders-of-magnitude rise over delta. Model simulations reveal
                  <jats:italic>shortening of the eclipse phase</jats:italic>
                  (the time between cellular uptake of the virus and onset of infectious viral replication and shedding)
                  <jats:italic>alone can generate 3-5 orders-of-magnitude higher viral load within 2 days post initial infection</jats:italic>
                  . Higher viral replication rates by an infected cell can generate at most one order-of-magnitude rise in viral load, whereas higher cell infectability has minimal impact and lowers the viral load.
                </jats:p>
title: Modeling predicts mechanisms altered by mutations of the SARS-CoV-2 delta and omicron variants

reference.unstructured: A. Chen , T. Wessler , K. Daftari , K. Hinton , R. Boucher , R. Pickles , R. Freeman , S. Lai and M. Forest , “Modeling insights into SARS-CoV-2 respiratory tract infections prior to immune protection,” Biophys J, in revision, 2022.
